items.title: Jamie Dimon's Grip On US Credit Card Dominance Grows As JPMorgan Wins Apple Card Business From Goldman Sachs
items.description: Apple has selected JPMorgan Chase to take over the Apple Card from Goldman Sachs in a deal that will shift more than $20 billion in card balances, bolster JPMorgan's credit card dominance, and mark another step in Goldman's retreat from consumer banking.

items.title: Here's Why Goldman Sachs (GS) Fell More Than Broader Market
items.description: Goldman Sachs (GS) concluded the recent trading session at $941.02, signifying a -1.51% move from its prior day's close.

items.title: Banking: M&A activity will see 'very rapid' boost in 2026
items.description: Earnings season is kicking off for big banks — JPMorgan Chase (JPM), Bank of America (BAC), Citigroup (C), Morgan Stanley (MS), Wells Fargo (WFC), Goldman Sachs (GS) — throughout next week. Major US bank stocks notably ended 2025 at new record highs. Mendon Capital senior portfolio manager Anton Schutz explains how deregulation and M&A activity could present a promising environment for the banking sector. To watch more expert insights and analysis on the latest market action, check out more Market Domination Overtime.

items.title: JPMorgan to replace Goldman as Apple Card issuer
items.description: JPMorgan Chase and Apple on Wednesday announced a deal under which the bank will become the new issuer of the ​Apple Card, replacing Goldman Sachs, further expanding the biggest U.S. bank's credit ‌card franchise.  The deal would cement JPMorgan's position in the credit cards segment and mark another win for ‌CEO Jamie Dimon, under whose leadership the bank has become a dominant force in retail and investment banking.  The move is estimated to bring over $20 billion in card balances to Chase's platform once completed, the companies said.
